action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: tree, ship, lake, river, farmer, boat, road, bird
Adjectives: (this language has no adjectives)
Verbs (write them in this base form): carry, feed, hear, hold, chase
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a tree carry a ship
    three trees carry a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    three boats feed a ship  =>  puka peki pa nopanu
    two boats feed a lake  =>  nepe peki po nopanu
    three boats feed a tree  =>  nakine peki pa nopanu
    a farmer chase two roads  =>  po tiranu dude kedo
    two ships feed three ships  =>  pa pukanu peki po pukanu
    a road chase three farmers  =>  pa kedonu dude tira
    two trees carry a boat  =>  nopa noni po nakinenu
    three birds carry a ship  =>  puka noni pa retenu
    three rivers carry three lakes  =>  pa nepenu noni pa sunonu
    a ship hear two birds  =>  po retenu kadi puka
    three roads hold three rivers  =>  pa sunonu nikene pa tiranu

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 4 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 3 English sentences (so at most
   12 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 3 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to analyze the existing data to understand the language structure. Looking at the examples:
- "three boats feed a ship" = "puka peki pa nopanu"
- "two boats feed a lake" = "nepe peki po nopanu"
- "three boats feed a tree" = "nakine peki pa nopanu"

I can see patterns: "peki" seems to be the verb "feed", "pa" might be "a" (indefinite), "po" might be "the" (definite). The subject nouns have different forms: "puka" (three boats), "nepe" (two boats), "nakine" (three boats - different from puka!). This suggests the language marks number and possibly definiteness on nouns.

Let me test hypotheses about word order and agreement patterns.

PROBES:
1. a boat feed a lake
2. the boat feed the lake
3. two lakes feed a boat
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a boat feed a lake"  =>  nepe peki nopa
2. "the boat feed the lake"  =>  nepe peki nopa
3. "two lakes feed a boat"  =>  nopa peki po nepenu
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 4 done. Probes used: 3 of 12. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Interesting! "a boat feed a lake" and "the boat feed the lake" both give "nepe peki nopa" - so definiteness might not be marked on the subject in this position. But in probe 3, "two lakes feed a boat" = "nopa peki po nepenu" - here "po" appears before "nepenu" (boat), suggesting "po" marks definiteness on the OBJECT when it's singular.

Looking at the patterns:
- "nepe" = two boats (subject)
- "nopa" = lake (object, indefinite) or lakes (subject)
- "po" = appears before definite singular objects
- "pa" = appears before indefinite singular objects

Let me test more combinations to understand the case/number marking system.

PROBES:
1. a bird chase a farmer
2. the bird chase the farmer
3. two farmers carry a ship
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird chase a farmer"  =>  kedo dude rete
2. "the bird chase the farmer"  =>  kedo dude rete
3. "two farmers carry a ship"  =>  puka noni po kedonu
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 4 done. Probes used: 6 of 12. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I see clearer patterns:
- "a bird chase a farmer" and "the bird chase the farmer" both = "kedo dude rete" - subject definiteness not marked
- "two farmers carry a ship" = "puka noni po kedonu" - here "po" before "kedonu" (ship), and "kedonu" has "-nu" ending

Looking at all data:
- "puka" = three boats (subject), also "two farmers" (puka)
- "nepe" = two boats (subject), also "a boat" (nepe)
- "nakine" = three boats (subject) - different from puka!
- "nopa" = lake (object) or lakes (subject)

It seems the subject form changes based on number AND possibly the verb or object. Let me test if the "-nu" ending on objects is a case marker, and whether subjects have different forms.

PROBES:
1. a lake carry a tree
2. two trees feed a lake
3. three ships chase a boat
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a lake carry a tree"  =>  nakine noni nepe
2. "two trees feed a lake"  =>  nepe peki po nakinenu
3. "three ships chase a boat"  =>  nopa dude pa pukanu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 4 done. Probes used: 9 of 12. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I'm seeing the patterns more clearly:

Subject forms by number:
- Singular: varies by noun (nepe = boat, nakine = lake, kedo = bird, puka = farmer)
- Two: varies (nepe = two boats, puka = two farmers, nopa = two lakes, nopa = three ships??)
- Three: varies (puka = three boats, nakine = three boats, nakine = a lake??)

Object forms:
- Singular indefinite: base form (rete = bird, nepe = boat, nakine = lake, nepe = tree??)
- Singular definite: base + -nu (kedonu = farmer, pukanu = boat, retenu = bird, sunonu = river)
- Plural: base + -nu (nepenu = lakes, tiranu = farmers)

Word order: SUBJECT VERB OBJECT

The key insight: "po" marks definite singular objects, "pa" marks indefinite singular objects. Plural objects don't get an article marker.

Let me test the remaining patterns before the exam.

PROBES:
1. a farmer feed a bird
2. the farmer feed the bird
3. two birds hear a tree
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a farmer feed a bird"  =>  rete peki kedo
2. "the farmer feed the bird"  =>  rete peki kedo
3. "two birds hear a tree"  =>  nakine kadi po retenu

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. three boats feed a river
2. a tree chase three birds
3. two farmers chase three birds
4. three boats hear a river
5. two boats chase a farmer
6. three ships carry two trees
7. two birds carry three trees
8. a river hold two lakes
9. two trees hear two boats
10. three farmers hold a farmer

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Let me map out all the word forms:

Subject forms:
- Singular: nepe = boat, nakine = lake/tree, kedo = bird, rete = farmer, pa = ship/road
- Two: nepe = boat, puka = farmer, nakine = tree, nopa = lake/ship, puka = bird
- Three: puka = boat, nakine = boat, nopa = ship, pa = river, nakine = bird

Object forms:
- Singular indefinite: base form (no article marker)
- Singular definite: base + -nu (with "po" before it)
- Plural: base + -nu (no article marker)

Word order: SUBJECT VERB OBJECT

Let me translate each:
1. three boats feed a river = puka peki po nepenu
2. a tree chase three birds = nakine dude puka
3. two farmers chase three birds = puka dude puka
4. three boats hear a river = puka kadi po nepenu
5. two boats chase a farmer = nepe dude kedo
6. three ships carry two trees = nopa noni po nakinenu
7. two birds carry three trees = nakine noni po nakinenu
8. a river hold two lakes = pa nikene po nepenu
9. two trees hear two boats = nakine kadi po nepenu
10. three farmers hold a farmer = puka nikene po retenu

ANSWERS:
1. puka peki po nepenu
2. nakine dude puka
3. puka dude puka
4. puka kadi po nepenu
5. nepe dude kedo
6. nopa noni po nakinenu
7. nakine noni po nakinenu
8. pa nikene po nepenu
9. nakine kadi po nepenu
10. puka nikene po retenu
action.label: response
